content.problemStatement:
There are two players P1P_1P1‚Äã, P2P_2P2‚Äã playing a game on an array AAA which has nnn positive integers: A1,A2,‚Ä¶,AnA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_nA1‚Äã,A2‚Äã,‚Ä¶,An‚Äã. Both the players take turns alternatively, and P1P_1P1‚Äã goes first. The rules of the game are as follows:


In P1P_1P1‚Äã‚Äôs turn, he should choose any element AiA_iAi‚Äã which is ‚â•1\ge 1‚â•1, and subtract 111 from it.
In P2P_2P2‚Äã‚Äôs turn, he should choose any element AiA_iAi‚Äã which is ‚â•2\ge 2‚â•2, and subtract 222 from it.
The player who is not able to make a move loses.
Given the array AAA, find the number of different indices iii such that P1P_1P1‚Äã can subtract from AiA_iAi‚Äã in his first turn, and end up winning the game, if both the players play optimally after this.


Input Format

The first line of the input contains an integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of the test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains an integer nnn.
The second line of each test case contains nnn space-separated integers denoting the array AAA.


Output Format
For each test case, output an integer corresponding to the number of possible first moves of P1P_1P1‚Äã that lead him to win.
constraints: Sum of nnn over all the test cases doesn't exceed 10610^6106

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Testcase 1: The array is {1}. P1P_1P1‚Äã has no choice but to pick the element and subtract 1 from it. The resulting array is {0}, and now P2P_2P2‚Äã is unable to make a move. Hence P1P_1P1‚Äã wins, and there was only one winning first move. Hence the answer is 1.
Testcase 2: The array is {3}. P1P_1P1‚Äã has no choice but to pick the element and subtract 1 from it. The resulting array is {2}, and now P2P_2P2‚Äã has no choice but to subtract 2 from it. The resulting array is {0}, and now P1P_1P1‚Äã is unable to make a move. Hence P1P_1P1‚Äã loses. There is no starting move for P1P_1P1‚Äã which leads to his victory. Hence the answer is 0.
Testcase 3: The array is {1, 2}. P1P_1P1‚Äã has two choices. Either subtract 1 from 1 or subtract 1 from 2.

Suppose he subtracts 1 from the first element, then the resulting array is {0, 2}, and now P2P_2P2‚Äã has no choice but to subtract 2 from the second element. The resulting array is {0, 0}, and now P1P_1P1‚Äã is unable to make a move. Hence P1P_1P1‚Äã loses in this case.
But suppose he subtracts 1 from the second element, then the resulting array is {1, 1}, and now P2P_2P2‚Äã is unable to make a move. Hence P1P_1P1‚Äã wins in this case.

Thus, there is exactly one starting move for P1P_1P1‚Äã which leads to his victory. Hence the answer is 1.
